author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm CVI

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This Psalm also hath the title Allelujah prefixed to it: and this twice.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But some say, that one Allelujah belongeth to the end of the former Psalm, the other to the beginning of this.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And they assert, that all the Psalms bearing this title have Allelujah at the end, but not all at the beginning; so that they will not allow any Psalm which hath not Allelujah at the end, to have it at the beginning; supposing that what seemeth to belong to the commencement, really belongeth to the end of the former Psalm.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But until they persuade us by some sure proofs that this is true, we will follow the general custom, which, whenever it findeth Allelujah, attributes it to the same Psalm, at the head of which it is found.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there are very few copies (and I have found this in none of the Greek copies, which I have been able to inspect) which have Allelujah at the end of the CLth Psalm; after which there is no other which belongeth to the same canon.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But not even this could outweigh custom, although all the copies had it so.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it might be that, with some reference to the praise of God, the whole book of Psalms, which is said to consist of five books (for they say that the books severally end where it is written Amen, Amen), might be closed with this last Allelujah, after all that hath been sung; nor, on account of the end of the CLth Psalm, do I see that it is necessary that all the Psalms entitled Allelujah, should have Allelujah at the end.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But when there is a double Allelujah at the head of a Psalm, why as our Lord sometimes once, sometimes twice over, saith Amen, in the same way Allelujah may not sometimes be used once, sometimes twice, I know not: especially, since as in this CVth, both the Allelujahs are placed after the mark by which the number of the Psalm is described, whereas the one, if it belonged to the end of the former Psalm, ought to have been placed before the number; and the Allelujah which belonged to the Psalm of this number, should have been written after the number.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But perhaps even in this an ignorant habit hath prevailed, and some reason may be assigned of which we are as yet uninformed, so that the judgment of truth ought rather to be our guide than the prejudice of custom.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In the mean time, before we are fully instructed in this matter, whenever we find Allelujah written, whether once or twice, after the number of the Psalm, according to the most usual custom of the Church, we will ascribe it to that Psalm to which the same number is prefixed; confessing that we both believe the mysteries of all the titles in the Psalms, and of the order of the same Psalms, to be important, and that we have not yet been able, as we wish, to penetrate them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But I find these two Psalms, the CVth and CVIth so connected, that in one of them, the first, the people of God is praised in the person of the elect, of whom there is no complaint, whom I imagine to have been there in those with whom God was well pleased; but in the following Psalm those are mentioned among the same people who have provoked God; though the mercy of God was not wanting even to these.…This Psalm therefore beginneth like the former; “Confess ye unto the Lord.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But in that Psalm these words follow: “And call upon His Name:” whereas here, it is as follows, “For He is gracious, and His mercy endureth for ever” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore in this passage a confession of sins may be understood; for after a few verses we read, “We have sinned with our fathers, we have done amiss, and dealt wickedly;” but in the words, “For He is gracious, and His mercy endureth for ever,” there is chiefly the praise of God, and in His praise confession.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Although when any one confesses his sins, he ought to do so with praise of God; nor is a confession of sins a pious one, unless it be without despair, and with calling upon the mercy of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It therefore doth contain His praise, whether in words, when it calleth Him gracious and merciful, or in the feeling only, when he believeth this.…If that mercy be here understood, in respect of which no man can be happy without God; we may render it better, “for ever:” but if it be that mercy which is shown to the wretched, that they may either be consoled in misery, or even freed from it; it is better construed, “to the end of the world,” in which there will never be wanting wretched persons to whom that mercy may be shown.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Unless indeed any man ventured to say, that some mercy of God will not be wanting even to those who shall be condemned with the devil and his angels; not a mercy by which they may be freed from that condemnation, but that it may be in some degree softened for them: and that thus the mercy of God may be styled eternal, as exercised over their eternal misery.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Who can express the mighty acts of the Lord?” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Full of the consideration of the Divine works, while he entreateth His mercy, “Who,” he saith, “can express the mighty acts of the Lord, or make all His praises heard?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We must supply what was said above, to make the sense complete here, thus, “Who shall make all His praises heard?” that is, who is sufficient to make all His praises heard?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Shall make” them “heard,” he saith; that is, cause that they be heard; showing, that the mighty acts of the Lord and His praises are so to be spoken of, that they may be preached to those who hear them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But who can make “all,” heard?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it that as the next words are, “Blessed are they that alway keep judgment, and do righteousness in every time” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 3); he perhaps meant those praises of His, which are understood as His works in His commandments?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For it is God,” saith the Apostle, “who worketh in you,” …since He worketh in these things in a manner that cannot be spoken.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Who will do all His praises heard?” that is, who, when he hath heard them, doth all His praises? which are the works of His commandments.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As far as they are done, although all which are heard are not performed, He is to be praised, who “worketh in us both to will and to do of His good pleasure.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this reason, while he might have said, all His commandments, or, all the works of His commandments; he preferred saying, “His praises.”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But unless there were some difference between judgment and righteousness, we should not read in another Psalm, “Until righteousness turn again unto judgment.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Scripture, indeed, loveth to place these two words together; as, “Righteousness and judgment are the habitation of His seat;” and this, “He shall make thy righteousness as clear as the light, and thy judgment as the noon-day;” where there is apparently a repetition of the same sentiment.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And perhaps on account of the resemblance of signification one may be put for the other, either judgment for righteousness, or righteousness for judgment: yet, if they be spoken of in their proper sense, I doubt not that there is some difference; viz. that he is said to keep judgment who judgeth rightly, but he to do righteousness who acts righteously.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And I think that the verse, “Until righteousness turn again unto judgment,” may not absurdly be understood in this sense: that here also those are called blessed, who keep judgment in faith, and do righteousness in deed.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Next, since God justifieth, that is, maketh men righteous, by healing them from their iniquities, a prayer followeth: “Remember me, O Lord, according to the favour that Thou bearest unto Thy people” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 4): that is, that we may be among those with whom Thou art well pleased; since God is not well pleased with them all.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “O visit me with Thy salvation.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is the Saviour Himself, in whom sins are forgiven, and souls healed, that they may be able to keep judgment, and do righteousness; and since they who here speak know such men to be blessed, they pray for this themselves.…“Visit us,” then, “with Thy salvation,” that is, with Thy Christ.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “To see the felicity of Thy chosen, and to rejoice in the gladness of Thy people” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 5): that is, visit us for this reason with Thy salvation, that we may see the felicity of Thy chosen, and rejoice in the gladness of Thy people.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For “felicity” some copies read “sweetness;” as in the former passage, “For He is gracious;” where others read, “for He is sweet.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And it is the same word in the Greek, as is elsewhere read, “The Lord shall show sweetness:” which some have translated “felicity,” others “bounty.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what meaneth, “Visit us to see the felicity of Thy chosen:” that is, that happiness which Thou givest to Thine elect: except that we may not remain blind, as those unto whom it is said, “But now ye say we see: therefore your sin remaineth.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the Lord giveth sight to the blind, not by their own merits, but in the felicity He giveth to His chosen, which is the meaning of “the felicity of Thy chosen:” as, the help of my countenance, is not of myself, but is my God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And we speak of our daily bread, as ours, but we add, Give unto us.…“That Thou mayest be praised with Thine inheritance.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I wonder this verse hath been so interpreted in many copies, since the Greek phrase is one and the same in these three verses.…But since this seemeth a doubtful expression, if that sense be true according to which interpreters have preferred, “That Thou mayest be praised,” the two preceding verses also must be so understood, because, as I have said, there is one Greek expression in these three verses; so that the whole should be thus understood, “Visit us with Thy salvation, that Thou mayest see the felicity of Thy chosen;” that is, visit us for this purpose, that Thou mayest cause us to be there, and mayest see us there; that “Thou mayest rejoice in the gladness of Thy people,” that is, that Thou mayest be said to rejoice, since they rejoice in Thee; that “Thou mayest be praised with Thine inheritance,” that is, mayest be praised with it, since it may not be praised save for Thy sake.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But let us hear what they next confess: “we have sinned with our fathers: we have done amiss, and dealt wickedly” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What meaneth “with our fathers”?…“Our fathers,” he saith, “regarded not Thy wonders in Egypt” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 7); and many other things also, he doth relate of their sins.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or is, “we have sinned with our fathers,” to be understood as meaning, we have sinned like our fathers, that is, by imitating their sins?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If it be so, it should be supported by some example of this mode of expression: which did not occur to me when I sought on this occasion an instance of any one saying that he had sinned, or done anything, with another, whom he had imitated by a similar act after a long interval of time.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What meaneth then, “Our fathers understood not Thy wonders;” save this, they did not know what Thou didst wish to convince them of by these miracles?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What indeed, save life eternal, and a good, not temporal, but immutable, which is waited for only through endurance?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this reason they impatiently murmured, and provoked, and they asked to be blessed with present and fugitive blessings, “Neither were they mindful of the greatness of Thy mercy.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He reproveth both their understanding and memory.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Understanding there was need of, that they might meditate unto what eternal blessings God was calling them through these temporal ones; and of memory, that at least they might not forget the temporal wonders which had been wrought, and might faithfully believe, that by the same power which they had already experienced, God would free them from the persecutions of their enemies; whereas they forgot the aid which He had given them in Egypt, by means of such wonders, to crush their enemies.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And they provoked, as they went up to the sea, even to the Red Sea.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We ought especially to notice how the Scripture doth censure the not understanding that which ought to have been understood, and the not remembering that which ought to have been remembered; which men are unwilling to have ascribed to their own fault, for no other reason than that they may pray less, and be less humble unto God, in whose sight they should confess what they are, and might by praying for His aid, become what they are not.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is better to accuse even the sins of ignorance and negligence, that they may be done away with, than to excuse them, so that they remain; and it is better to clear them off by calling upon God, than to clench them by provoking Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He addeth, that God acted not according to their unbelief.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Nevertheless,” he saith, “He saved them for His Name’s sake: that He might make His power to be known” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 8): not on account of any deservings of their own.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “He rebuked the Red Sea also, and it was dried up” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We do not read that any voice was sent forth from Heaven to rebuke the sea; but he hath called the Divine Power by which this was effected, a rebuke: unless indeed any one may choose to say, that the sea was secretly rebuked, so that the waters might hear, and yet men could not.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The power by which God acteth is very abstruse and mysterious, a power which He causeth that even things devoid of sense instantly obey at His will.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “So He led them through the deeps, as through a wilderness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He calleth a multitude of waters the deeps.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For some wishing to give the sense of this whole verse, have translated, “So He led them forth amid many waters.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What then doth “through the deeps, as through a wilderness,” mean, except that that had become as a wilderness from its dryness, where before had been the watery deeps?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And He saved them from the hating ones” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Some translators, in order to avoid an expression unusual in Latin, have rendered the word, by a circumlocution, “And He saved them from the hand of those that hated them, and redeemed them from the hand of the enemy.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What price was given in this redemption?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it a prophecy, since this deed was a figure of Baptism, wherein we are redeemed from the hand of the devil at a great price, which price is the Blood of Christ? whence this is more consistently figured forth, not by any sea indiscriminately, but by the Red Sea; since blood hath a red colour.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “As for those that troubled them, the waters overwhelmed them: there was not one of them left” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 11); not of all the Egyptians, but of those who pursued the departing Israelites, desirous either of taking or of killing them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Then believed they in His words” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The expression seemeth barely Latin, for he saith not “believed His word,” or “on His words,” but “in His words;” yet it is very frequent in Scripture.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And praised praise unto Him;” such an expression as when we say, “This servitude he served,” “such a life he lived.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He is here alluding to that well-known hymn, commencing, “I will sing unto the Lord, for He hath triumphed gloriously: the horse and the rider hath He thrown into the sea.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They acted hastily: they forgot His works” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 13): other copies read more intelligibly, “They hastened, they forgot His works, and would not abide His counsel.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they ought to have thought, that so great works of God towards themselves were not without a purpose, but that they invited them to some endless happiness, which was to be waited for with patience; but they hastened to make themselves happy with temporal things, which give no man true happiness, because they do not quench insatiable longing: for “whosoever,” saith our Lord, “shall drink of this water, shall thirst again.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Lastly, “And they lusted a lust in the wilderness, and they tempted God in the dry land” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The “dry land,” or land without water, and “desert,” are the same: so also are, “they lusted a lust,” and, “they tempted God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The form of speech is the same as above, “they praised a praise.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And He gave them their desire, and sent fulness withal into their souls” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But He did not thus render them happy: for it was not that fulness of which it is said, “Blessed are they which do hunger and thirst after righteousness: for they shall be filled.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In this passage he doth not speak of the rational soul, but of the soul as giving animal life to the body; to the substance of which belong meat and drink, according to what is said in the Gospel, “Is not the soul more than meat, and the body than raiment?” as if it belonged to the soul to eat, to the body to be clothed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And they angered Moses in the tents, and Aaron the saint of the Lord” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What angering, or, as some have more literally rendered it, what provocation, he speaketh of, the following words sufficiently show.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The earth opened,” he saith, “and swallowed up Dathan, and covered over the congregation of Abiram” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 17): “swallowed up” answereth to “covered over.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Both Dathan and Abiram were equally concerned in a most sacrilegious schism.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And the fire was kindled in their company; the flame burnt up the sinners” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This word is not in Scripture usually applied to those, who, although they live righteously, and in a praiseworthy manner, are not without sin.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Rather, as there is a difference between those who scorn and scorners, between men who murmur and murmurers, between men who are writing and writers, and so forth; so Scripture is wont to signify by sinners such as are very wicked, and laden with heavy loads of sins.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And they made a calf in Horeb, and worshipped the graven image” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Thus they changed their glory, in the similitude of a calf that eateth hay” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He saith not “into” the likeness, but “in” the likeness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is such a form of speech as where he said “and they believed in His words.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: With great effect in truth he saith not, they changed the glory of God when they did this; as the Apostle also saith, “They changed the glory of the incorruptible God into an image made like to corruptible man:” but “their glory.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For God was their glory, if they would abide His counsel, and hasten not.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They forgat God who saved them” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How did He save them?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Who did so great things in Egypt: Wondrous works in the land of Ham, and fearful things in the Red Sea” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The things that are wondrous, are also fearful; for there is no wonder without a certain fear: although these might be called fearful, because they beat down their adversaries, and showed them what they ought to fear.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “So He said, He would have destroyed them” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Since they forgot Him who saved them, the Worker of wondrous works, and made and worshipped a graven image, by this atrocious and incredible impiety they deserved death.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Had not Moses His chosen stood before Him in the breaking.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He doth not say, that he stood in the breaking, as if to break the wrath of God, but in the way of the breaking, meaning the stroke which was to strike them: that is, had he not put himself in the way for them, saying, “Yet now, if Thou wilt forgive their sin;—and if not, blot me, I pray Thee, out of Thy book.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Where it is proved how greatly the intercession of the saints in behalf of others prevaileth with God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For Moses, fearless in the justice of God, which could not blot him out, implored mercy, that He would not blot out those whom He justly might.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus he “stood before Him in the breaking, to turn away His wrathful indignation, lest He should destroy them.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Yea, they thought scorn of that pleasant land” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But had they seen it?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How then could they scorn that which they had not seen, except as the following words explain, “and believed not in His words.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Indeed, unless that land which was styled the land that flowed with milk and honey, signified something great, through which, as by a visible token, He was leading those who understood His wondrous works to invisible grace and the kingdom of heaven, they could not be blamed for scorning that land, whose temporal kingdom we also ought to esteem as nothing, that we may love that Jerusalem which is free, the mother of us all, which is in heaven, and truly to be desired.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But rather unbelief is here reproved, since they gave no credence to the words of God, who was leading them to great things through small things, and hastening to bless themselves with temporal things, which they carnally savoured of, they “abided not His counsel,” as is said above.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But murmured in their tents, and hearkened not unto the voice of the Lord” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 25); who strongly forbade them to murmur.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Then lift He up His hand against them, to overthrow them in the wilderness” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 26); “to cast out their seed among the nations: and to scatter them in the lands” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “They were initiated also unto Baalpeor;” that is, were consecrated to the Gentile idol; “and ate the offerings of the dead” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Thus they provoked Him to anger with their own inventions; and destruction was multiplied among them” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As if He had deferred the lifting up of His hand which was to cast them down in the desert, and to cast out their seed among the nations, and to scatter them in the lands; as the Apostle saith: “And even as they did not like to retain God in their knowledge, God gave them over to a reprobate mind, to do those things which are not convenient.” “‘Destruction,’ therefore, ‘was multiplied among them,’ when they were heavily punished for their heavy sins.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Then stood up Phineas, and appeased Him, and the shaking ceased” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He hath related the whole briefly, because he is not here teaching the ignorant, but reminding those who know the history.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The word “shaking” here is the same as “breaking” before.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is one word in the Greek.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Lastly, so great was their wickedness, in being consecrated to the idol, and eating the sacrifices of the dead (that is, because the Gentiles sacrificed to dead men as to God), that God would not be otherwise appeased than as Phineas the Priest appeased Him, when he slew a man and a woman together whom he found in adultery.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If he had done this from hatred towards them, and not from love, while zeal for the house of God devoured him, it would not have been counted unto him for righteousness.…Christ our Lord indeed, when the New Testament was revealed, chose a milder discipline; but the threat of hell is more severe, and this we do not read of in those threatenings held out by God in His temporal government.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And that was counted unto him for righteousness among all posterities for evermore” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: God counted this unto His Priest for righteousness, not only as long as posterity shall exist, but “for evermore;” for He who knoweth the heart, knoweth how to weigh with how much love for the people that deed was done.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And they angered Him at the waters of strife: so that Moses was vexed for their sakes” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 32); “because they provoked his spirit, so that he spake doubtfully with his lips” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is spake doubtfully?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As if God, who had done so great wonders before, could not cause water to flow from a rock.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he touched the rock with his rod with doubt, and thus distinguished this miracle from the rest, in which he had not doubted.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He thus offended, thus deserved to hear that he should die, without entering into the land of promise.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For being disturbed by the murmurs of an unbelieving people, he held not fast that confidence which he ought to have held.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Nevertheless, God giveth unto him, as unto His chosen, a good testimony even after his death, so that we may see that this wavering of faith was punished with this penalty only, that he was not allowed to enter that land, whither he was leading the people.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But they of whose iniquities this Psalm speaketh, when they had entered into that temporal land of promise, “destroyed not the heathen, which the Lord commanded them” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 34); “but were mingled among the heathen, and learned their works” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Insomuch that they worshipped their idols, which became to them an offence” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Their not destroying them, but mingling with them, became to them an offence.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Yea, they offered their sons and their daughters unto devils” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 37); “and shed innocent blood, even the blood of their sons and of their daughters, whom they offered unto the idols of Canaan” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That history doth not relate that they offered their sons and daughters to devils and idols; but neither can that Psalm lie, nor the Prophets, who assert this in many passages of their rebukes.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But the literature of the Gentiles is not silent respecting this custom of theirs.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what is it that followeth?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And the land was slain with bloods.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We might suppose that this was a mistake of the writer, and that he had written interfecta for infecta, were it not for the goodness of God, who hath willed His Scriptures to be written in many languages; were it not that we see it written as in the text in many Greek copies which we have inspected; “the land was slain with bloods.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What meaneth then, “the land was slain,” unless this be referred to the men who dwelt in the land, by a metaphorical expression.…For they themselves were slaying their own souls when they offered up their sons, and when they shed the blood of infants who were far from consent to this crime: whence it is said, “They shed innocent blood.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The land” therefore “was slain with bloods, and defiled by their works” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 39), since they themselves were slain in soul, and defiled by their works; “and they went a whoring after their own inventions.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: By inventions are meant what the Greeks call ἐπιτηδεύματα: for this word doth occur in the Greek copies both in this and a former passage, where it is said, “They provoked Him to anger with their own inventions;” “inventions” in both instances signifying what they had initiated others in.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let no man therefore suppose inventions to mean what they had of themselves instituted, without any example before them to imitate.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence other translators in the Latin tongue have preferred pursuits, affections, imitations, pleasures, to inventions: and the very same who here write inventions, have elsewhere written pursuits.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: I chose to mention this, lest the word inventions, applied to what they had not invented, but imitated from others, might raise a difficulty.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Therefore was the wrath of the Lord kindled against His own people” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Our translators have been unwilling to use the word anger, for the Greek θυμὸς; though some have used it; while others translate by “indignation” or “mind.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whichever of these terms be adopted, passion doth not affect God; but the power of punishing hath assumed this name metaphorically from custom.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Insomuch that He abhorred His own inheritance; and He gave them over into the hand of the heathen: and they that hated them were lords over them” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 41): “and their enemies oppressed them, and they were brought low under their hands” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Since he hath called them the inheritance of God, it is clear that He abhorred them, and gave them over into their enemies’ hands, not in order to their perdition, but for their discipline.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Lastly, he saith, “Many a time did He deliver them.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But they provoked Him with their own counsels” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is what he said above, “They did not abide His counsel.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now a man’s counsel is pernicious to himself, when he seeketh those things which are his own only, not those which are God’s.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In whose inheritance, which inheritance He Himself is to us, when He deigneth His presence for our enjoyment, being with the Saints, we shall suffer no straitening from the society, by our love of anything as our own possession.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For that most glorious city, when it hath gained the promised inheritance, in which none shall die, none shall be born, will not contain citizens who shall individually rejoice in their own, for “God shall be all in all.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And whoever in this pilgrimage faithfully and earnestly doth long for this society, doth accustom himself to prefer common to private interests, by seeking not his own things, but Jesus Christ’s: lest, by being wise and vigilant in his own affairs, he provoke God with his own counsel; but, hoping for what he seeth not, let him not hasten to be blessed with things visible; and, patiently waiting for that everlasting happiness which he seeth not, follow His counsel in His promises, whose aid he prayeth for in his prayers.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus he will also become humble in his confessions; so as not to be like those, of whom it is said, “They were brought down in their wickedness.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Nevertheless, God, full of mercy, forsook them not.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And He saw when they were in adversity, when He heard their complaint” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And He thought upon His covenant, and repented, according to the multitude of His mercies” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He saith, “He repented,” because He changed that wherewith He seemed about to destroy them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: With God indeed all things are arranged and fixed; and when He seemeth to act upon sudden motive, He doth nothing but what He foreknew that He should do from eternity; but in the temporal changes of creation, which He ruleth wonderfully, He, without any temporal change in Himself, is said to do by a sudden act of will what in the ordained causes of events He hath arranged in the unchangeableness of His most secret counsel, according to which He doth everything according to defined seasons, doing the present, and having already done the future.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And who is capable of comprehending these things?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let us therefore hear the Scripture, speaking high things humbly, giving food for the nourishment of children, and proposing subjects for the research of the older: that everlasting covenant “which He made with Abraham,” not the old which is abolished, but the new which is hidden even in the old.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And pitied them,” etc. He did that which He had covenanted, but He had foreknown that He would yield this to them when they prayed in their adversity; since even their very prayer, when it was not uttered, but was still to be uttered, undoubtedly was known unto God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: So “He gave them unto compassions, in the sight of all that had taken them captive” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That they might not be vessels of wrath, but vessels of mercy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The compassions unto which He gave them are named in the plural for this reason, I imagine, because each one hath a gift of his own from God, one in one way, another in another.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Come then, whosoever readest this, and dost recognise the grace of God, by which we are redeemed unto eternal life through our Lord Jesus Christ, by reading in the apostolical writings, and by searching in the Prophets, and seest the Old Testament revealed in the New, the New veiled in the Old; remember the words of our Lord Jesus Christ, where, when He driveth him out of the hearts of the faithful, He saith, “Now is the prince of this world cast out:” and again of the Apostle, when he saith, “Who hath delivered us from the power of darkness, and hath translated us into the kingdom of His dear Son.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Meditate on these and such like things, examine also the Old Testament, and see what is sung in that Psalm, the title of which is, When the temple was being built after the captivity: for there it is said, “Sing unto the Lord a new song.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And, that thou mayest not think it doth refer to the Jewish people only, he saith, “Sing unto the Lord, all the whole earth: sing unto the Lord, and praise His Name: declare,” or rather, “give the good news of,” or, to transfer the very word used in the Greek, “evangelize day from day, His salvation.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Here the Gospel (Evangelium) is mentioned, in which is announced the Day that came from Day, our Lord Christ, the Light from Light, the Son from the Father.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This also is the meaning of His salvation: for Christ is the Salvation of God, as we have shown above.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Deliver us, O Lord our God, and gather us from among the nations (other copies read, “from the heathen”); that we may give thanks unto Thy holy Name, and make our boast of Thy praise” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Then he hath briefly added this very praise, “Blessed be the Lord God of Israel from everlasting, and world without end” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 48): by which we understand from everlasting to everlasting; because He shall be praised without end by those of whom it is said, “Blessed are they that dwell in Thy house: they will be alway praising Thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is the perfection of the Body of Christ on the third day, when the devils had been cast out, and cures perfected, even unto the immortality of the body itself, the everlasting reign of those who perfectly praise Him, because they perfectly love Him; and perfectly love Him, because they behold Him face to face.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For then shall be completed the prayer at the commencement of this Psalm: “Remember us, O Lord, according to the favour that Thou bearest unto Thy people,” etc. For from the Gentiles He doth not gather only the lost sheep of the house of Israel, but also those which do not belong to that fold; so that there is one flock, as is said, and one Shepherd.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But when the Jews suppose that that prophecy belongeth to their visible kingdom, because they know not how to rejoice in the hope of good things unseen, they are about to rush into the snares of him, of whom the Lord saith, “I am come in My Father’s Name, and ye receive Me not: if another shall come in his own name, him ye will receive.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Of whom the Apostle Paul saith: “that Man of Sin shall be revealed, the son of perdition,” etc. And a little after he saith, “Then shall that Wicked be revealed, whom the Lord shall consume with the Spirit of His mouth, and shall destroy with the brightness of His coming,” etc.…Through that Apostate, through him who exalteth himself above all that is called God, or that is worshipped, it seemeth to me, that the carnal people of Israel will suppose that prophecy to be fulfilled, where it is said, “Deliver us, O Lord, and gather us from among the heathen;” that under His guidance, before the eyes of their visible enemies, who had visibly taken them captive, they are to have visible glory.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thus they will believe a lie, because they have not received the love of truth, that they might love not carnal, but spiritual blessings.…For Christ had other sheep that were not of this fold: but the devil and his angels had taken captive all those sheep, both among the Israelites and the Gentiles.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The power, therefore, of the devil having been cast out of them, in the sight of the evil spirits who had taken them captive, their cry in this prophecy is, that they may be saved and perfected for evermore: “Deliver us, O Lord our God, and gather us from among the heathen.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Not, as the Jews imagine it, fulfilled through Antichrist, but through our Lord Christ coming in the name of His Father, “Day from day, His salvation;” of whom it is here said, “O visit us in Thy salvation!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And let all the people say,” the predestined people of the circumcision and of the uncircumcision, a holy race, an adopted people, “So be it!
